rules.description: Missing unprivileged UID map (uidmap not set)
rules.risk: Without a UID map, the container runs in the host user namespace. UID 0 inside the container is UID 0 on the host, enabling direct host compromise on escape.
rules.remediation: Add 'lxc.idmap = u 0 <host_start> <range>' to the LXC config to enable user namespace isolation.

rules.description: Missing unprivileged GID map (gidmap not set)
rules.risk: Without a GID map, the container shares the host group namespace. Group-based file permissions on the host become exploitable from within the container.
rules.remediation: Add 'lxc.idmap = g 0 <host_start> <range>' to the LXC config to enable group namespace isolation.

rules.description: UID map format invalid (should be '0 <non-zero> <non-zero>')
rules.risk: A malformed UID map may silently disable user namespace isolation, making the container effectively privileged.
rules.remediation: Correct the lxc.idmap line to format: 'u 0 <host_uid_start> <range>' where host_uid_start and range are non-zero integers.

rules.description: GID map format invalid (should be '0 <non-zero> <non-zero>')
rules.risk: A malformed GID map may silently disable group namespace isolation, making host group permissions exploitable.
rules.remediation: Correct the lxc.idmap line to format: 'g 0 <host_gid_start> <range>' where host_gid_start and range are non-zero integers.

rules.description: No capabilities are dropped (lxc.cap.drop not set)
rules.risk: The container retains all default Linux capabilities. Capabilities such as CAP_NET_RAW, CAP_SYS_PTRACE increase the attack surface.
rules.remediation: Add 'lxc.cap.drop = <capability_list>' to drop unnecessary capabilities. Consider dropping all and adding back only required ones.

rules.description: Mounting /proc is dangerous
rules.risk: Host /proc exposes process information, kernel parameters, and memory maps. Attackers can read credentials from /proc/*/environ or manipulate kernel tunables.
rules.remediation: Remove the lxc.mount.entry for /proc. If required, use lxc.mount.auto with restricted proc (e.g., proc:mixed).

rules.description: Mounting /sys is dangerous
rules.risk: Host /sys provides writable access to kernel subsystems (cgroups, devices, network). Attackers can reconfigure cgroups to escape the container.
rules.remediation: Remove the lxc.mount.entry for /sys. If required, mount as read-only (add 'ro' to options).

rules.description: Mounting /run is dangerous
rules.risk: Host /run contains runtime state including D-Bus sockets, systemd control sockets, and container engine sockets. Access enables privilege escalation and lateral movement.
rules.remediation: Remove the lxc.mount.entry for /run. Provide only specific sockets or paths if needed.

rules.description: Mounting /usr should be readonly
rules.risk: A writable /usr mount allows the container to replace host system binaries, enabling backdoor installation or supply-chain attacks.
rules.remediation: Add 'ro' to the mount options for the /usr mount entry.

rules.description: Mounting /dev (or subpaths) should be readonly
rules.risk: Writable access to host /dev allows direct device manipulation. Attackers can read raw disk, write to block devices, or create device nodes for escape.
rules.remediation: Add 'ro' to the mount options for /dev mount entries. Prefer bind-mounting only specific devices needed.

rules.description: LXC container is running as root
rules.risk: The container process runs as UID 0 in the host namespace. Any container escape grants immediate host root access.
rules.remediation: Configure unprivileged UID/GID maps (lxc.idmap) and ensure the systemd service starts with a non-root User directive.

rules.description: AppArmor profile is set to unconfined
rules.risk: Disabling AppArmor removes mandatory access control enforcement, allowing the container process unrestricted access to system resources within its namespace.
rules.remediation: Remove 'lxc.apparmor.profile = unconfined'. Use the default AppArmor profile or provide a custom profile tailored to the workload.

rules.description: Container shares the host network namespace
rules.risk: Host network namespace sharing gives the container full access to the host network stack, including listening on privileged ports, sniffing traffic, and bypassing network-level container isolation.
rules.remediation: Set 'lxc.net.0.type' to 'veth' or another isolated network type instead of 'host' or 'none'.

rules.description: lxc.no_new_privs is not enabled
rules.risk: Without no_new_privs, processes inside the container can gain more privileges than their parent via setuid binaries or kernel exploits.
rules.remediation: Add 'lxc.no_new_privs = 1' to the LXC container configuration.

rules.description: No seccomp profile is configured
rules.risk: Without a seccomp profile, the container process can invoke any syscall the kernel allows, expanding the attack surface for kernel exploits.
rules.remediation: Add 'lxc.seccomp.profile = /usr/share/lxc/config/common.seccomp' or a custom seccomp profile to the LXC configuration.

rules.description: lxc.cap.keep retains dangerous capabilities
rules.risk: Keeping dangerous capabilities such as sys_admin, sys_ptrace, sys_module, net_raw, or net_admin enables kernel module loading, process tracing, network-level attacks, and container escape.
rules.remediation: Remove dangerous capabilities from lxc.cap.keep. Only keep the minimum capabilities required by the workload.

rules.description: Container shares namespaces with other containers or host via lxc.namespace.share.*
rules.risk: Explicit namespace sharing completely bypasses container isolation for the shared namespace. A container sharing PID namespace can signal or ptrace processes in the target; sharing network namespace exposes the target's network stack.
rules.remediation: Remove all lxc.namespace.share.* directives. Use network bridges or explicit IPC mechanisms instead of namespace sharing.

rules.description: Container keeps host namespaces via lxc.namespace.keep (isolation bypassed)
rules.risk: lxc.namespace.keep prevents the container from creating its own namespaces for the specified types. The container shares the host's namespace directly, breaking isolation completely for those resources.
rules.remediation: Remove lxc.namespace.keep directive. Allow LXC to create isolated namespaces for the container.

rules.description: lxc.mount.auto contains dangerous writable mounts (proc:rw, sys:rw, or cgroup:rw)
rules.risk: Writable auto-mounts of /proc, /sys, or cgroups enable direct kernel parameter modification and cgroup escape. An attacker can write to /proc/sys to disable security mitigations or manipulate cgroups to break out of the container.
rules.remediation: Change lxc.mount.auto to use read-only variants (e.g., 'proc:mixed' instead of 'proc:rw', 'sys:ro' instead of 'sys:rw'). Never mount cgroups as writable.

rules.description: SELinux context is unconfined or not configured
rules.risk: Without a proper SELinux context, the container process has no mandatory access control restrictions. On SELinux-enforcing systems, an unconfined container can access any resource permitted by DAC alone.
rules.remediation: Set 'lxc.selinux.context' to a confined type (e.g., 'system_u:system_r:lxc_t:s0'). Avoid 'unconfined_t' or empty values.

rules.description: Dangerous AppArmor nesting or raw profile directives detected
rules.risk: lxc.apparmor.allow_nesting, allow_incomplete, or raw directives weaken the AppArmor confinement, enabling nested containers to escape the outer AppArmor policy or inject arbitrary profile rules.
rules.remediation: Remove lxc.apparmor.allow_nesting, lxc.apparmor.allow_incomplete, and lxc.apparmor.raw directives. Use a properly scoped AppArmor profile instead.

rules.description: Seccomp nesting is enabled, weakening privilege boundary
rules.risk: lxc.seccomp.allow_nesting permits nested containers to define their own seccomp profiles, potentially allowing syscalls blocked by the outer profile and widening the kernel attack surface.
rules.remediation: Remove 'lxc.seccomp.allow_nesting = 1' from the LXC configuration. Enforce seccomp profiles at the outermost container level.

rules.description: Unrestricted cgroup device access (lxc.cgroup.devices.allow = a or broad patterns)
rules.risk: Allowing all devices or broad device patterns (e.g., 'a', 'c *:* rwm') gives the container raw access to host block and character devices, enabling disk read/write, GPU access, and container escape.
rules.remediation: Replace broad cgroup device allow rules with specific device entries. Only allow the exact devices the container workload requires.

rules.description: No memory limit configured (lxc.cgroup.memory.limit_in_bytes or lxc.cgroup2.memory.max)
rules.risk: Without a memory limit, a compromised or misbehaving container can consume all host memory, causing denial of service for other containers and host processes.
rules.remediation: Add 'lxc.cgroup.memory.limit_in_bytes = <bytes>' or 'lxc.cgroup2.memory.max = <bytes>' to the LXC configuration.

rules.description: No process limit configured (lxc.prlimit.nproc)
rules.risk: Without a process limit, a compromised container can fork-bomb the host, exhausting PIDs and causing denial of service for all workloads.
rules.remediation: Add 'lxc.prlimit.nproc = <max_processes>' to the LXC configuration to limit the number of processes the container can create.

rules.description: Container rootfs is not mounted read-only
rules.risk: A writable rootfs allows attackers to modify container binaries, install backdoors, or tamper with application files. Read-only rootfs forces explicit writable volume declarations.
rules.remediation: Add 'lxc.rootfs.options = ro' to the LXC configuration to mount the container rootfs as read-only.

rules.description: LXC container has no corresponding systemd service for automatic lifecycle management
rules.risk: In embedded/automotive environments all container services must be managed by systemd. A container without a systemd service will not auto-start at boot and has no supervised restart behavior.
rules.remediation: Create a systemd service unit file with 'ExecStart=/usr/bin/lxc-start -n <container_name>' to manage the LXC container lifecycle.

rules.description: Systemd service does not restrict capabilities via CapabilityBoundingSet
rules.risk: Without CapabilityBoundingSet in the systemd unit, the lxc-start process inherits all capabilities from its parent, increasing the blast radius if the container is compromised.
rules.remediation: Add 'CapabilityBoundingSet=' to the [Service] section of the systemd unit to restrict the daemon to only the capabilities it requires.

rules.description: Mount entry uses dangerous options (rbind, loop, or remount)
rules.risk: rbind recursively exposes parent mount tree including /proc. loop enables mounting arbitrary disk images. remount can change read-only mounts to writable, bypassing protections.
rules.remediation: Remove rbind, loop, and remount from mount options. Use specific bind mounts with minimal scope.

rules.description: LXC binaries detected inside container rootfs (nested LXC-in-LXC)
rules.risk: Nested containers add a second privilege boundary with compounded complexity. The inner container may bypass outer container security restrictions, especially if AppArmor nesting is enabled.
rules.remediation: Remove LXC binaries from the container rootfs unless nested containerization is explicitly required and properly hardened.
